column18:
Town Hall Meeting Survey
Informed Consent

Hello Chico Student:

The following online survey is part of an important university study on how to improve students' experiences at the Town Hall Meeting. It will take approximately 5-10 minutes to complete.

Taking part in this research is completely voluntary. If you choose to participate in this research, it is very important that you answer the questions as thoughtfully and frankly as possible. All your answers will be kept strictly confidential. You can stop participating at any time without penalty to your grade.
We also emphasize that you will NOT be penalized if you choose to omit answers to some questions on the survey. To record your answers, you must proceed all the way through the end of the survey, even if you choose to omit answers to some questions. In addition, proceeding to the very end of the survey will remove any survey response information from the screen on your computer/device, protecting your privacy. 

Your student ID is being collected so we can match your responses to other important university programs (U-Course, Town Hall Meeting, Great Debate) and demographic information. IDs may be used for statistical analysis alongside other data such as GPA, retention, and graduation rates. Again, your answers are strictly confidential, and we are bound by the rules of this University to maintain this confidentiality. Your information collected as part of this research will be stored on a password-protected computer in a locked office within the First-Year Experience Program office and on a protected server that is located behind a secure firewall at the University Office for Institutional Research. You must be 18 years of age or older to take this survey.

Thank you for filling out this survey as completely and honestly as you can. We are asking you to do this because we value your experience and ideas about how to improve students' experiences here at Chico State.

If you'd like more information about this project, contact the First-Year Experience office at fye@csuchico.edu or (530) 898-3705.

It also helped me to see that we as individuals have the power to create change and that we can reach out to elected officials. I used to think that there was no way to be able to share my ideas with them but town hall helped me to see that they want to hear what we have to say and also gave me ideas of how to get in contact with these officials. This experience and class showed me how interesting government is and so I added a minor in political science to hopefully explore more in this field.

column65:
Hey! I had this town hall meeting recently, and it was such an incredible experience. We were discussing climate change and environmental issues, and my focus was on wildfire prevention and forest management. I’ll admit, I was a little nervous at first, but once I started talking about sustainable forest practices and the importance of controlled burns, it all just clicked, and I felt so confident.

What made it even better was the format—it wasn’t just me presenting. I got to sit down with a group of people who were also passionate about climate issues. We all had similar goals but approached the topic from different perspectives, which made the conversation so dynamic. While I focused on wildfire prevention, others brought up ideas like renewable energy initiatives and policies to cut carbon emissions. It was fascinating to see how our ideas connected while also complementing each other in unique ways.

We had some great discussions, even debating the best strategies for creating meaningful, long-term change. It didn’t feel intimidating at all—it was more like a collaborative brainstorming session where everyone’s input mattered. I think the best part was realizing how much potential there is for teamwork in tackling these complex issues. I left feeling inspired and excited, with plenty of fresh ideas to explore further!

You go to one table, listen to the person, write down what you learn, get a stamp, and then move on to two more different tables and do the same thing. After you turn in your paper and you're all done.

column65: The event definitely had it ups and its downs. Leading up to the event, I really enjoyed researching my policy topic and watching all of the informational videos on what the event would be like. At the actual event, I felt really inspired by the students in my same policy category because it made me feel like I wasn't the only one who thought there were issues within higher education. However, as we shared our research and our problems and solutions, the lady from the criminal justice department (I believe she was our consultant - definitely not the guy from grad studies) seemed to just shut all of the students down. She told us that the only reason why we chose this policy topic was because we were students and that there was no way we would be able to convince taxpayers to pay for an education that wasn't going to benefit them.
